data.title: The Dividend Strategy That Could Make Me A Millionaire - And How You Can Copy It
data.description:
Introduction

Everyone is rich.

At least, that's what it seems like.

According to a Business Insider article last year, using Federal Reserve data, the average American family has a $1.06 million net worth. In other words, it's fair to say the average American family is a millionaire.

Now comes the bad news, which is that the very wealthy massively skew these numbers. After all, if Elon Musk were to enter a homeless shelter, the average net worth inside the building would be way above $1 billion per person.

According to the same source, the median net worth is $192,900, which means that half of all households have more than that, while the other half has less than that. It's a much better measurement of wealth.

The good news is that $192,900 isn't bad at all, especially because younger people drag down this number. As we can see, people above the age of 45 have a median net worth of roughly $250,000, a very decent number to build even more long-term wealth — for whatever purpose.

With that said, I want to focus on the people whose net worth has at least seven digits, as there's a lot to discuss about America's millionaires. I know that quite a few people who read this are part of that group.

Last year, Ramsey Solutions conducted a survey of 10,000 millionaires, which found some fascinating stuff.

  • 8 out of 10 millionaires invested in their company's 401(k)
  • All kept highly diversified portfolios, as no millionaire said that single-stock investing was a big factor in their financial success.

On the one hand, I was a bit shocked that single stocks were not key. On the other hand, that's also no surprise, as the stock market has been very kind to people, allowing investors to turn $10,000 into almost $74,000 by simply staying invested in the S&P 500 for 20 years (see below). While some stock pickers may have done much better, the average “buy and hold” investor did quite well without taking on any single-stock risks.

This brings me to the next bullet point.

  • 3 out of 4 millionaires said that regular, consistent investing over a long period of time leads to success.

As obvious as this may sound, the average millionaire doesn't live the way you may expect a millionaire to live. I added emphasis to the quote below:

Ninety-four percent of the people studied said they live on less than they make, and nearly three-quarters of the millionaires have never carried a credit card balance in their lives!

These millionaires also said they spend $200 or less each month at restaurants. And 93% of millionaires use coupons all or some of the time when shopping. By staying out of debt and watching expenses, they’re able to build their bank accounts instead of trying to get out of a financial hole every month. - Ramsey Solutions.

With all of this in mind, while some stats may be a bit obvious, they make sense, as the majority (79%) of millionaires did not receive any inheritance at all from their parents and/or other family members. Only 3% of all millionaires inherited $1 million or more.

So far, we have discussed three things:

  • Almost all millionaires are self-made.
  • Most millionaires live like non-millionaires.
  • A lot of wealth was built by consistent investing.

But wait, it gets better.

Most millionaires didn't even make a lot of money, as only 15% of millionaires were in senior leadership roles, like vice president or C/suite roles like CEO, CFO, COO, and others.

  • Only 31% averaged $100,000 a year over the course of their careers, with the top five careers being engineer, accountant, teacher, management, and attorney.

With that said, I would take the salary with a grain of salt. Due to inflation, I believe to compete with the people who became millionaires with $100,000 salaries, one needs to make at least $130,000-$150,000. The purchasing power of $100,000 in the year 2000 has dropped so much that one needs more than $180,000 in 2025 dollars to keep up. Assuming that most were gradually investing, I went with $130,000-$150,000 as a comparable number.

This brings me to my strategy and how I want to achieve the same outcome.

What I'm Doing To Build Wealth

And how to copy my strategy.

24 years. That's how long it takes to become a millionaire if you invest a lump sum of $10,000, followed by monthly investments of $1,000 with an annual return of 9%, which is below the market's 10-11% long-term annual return.

After 30 years, you'll have roughly $1.8 million.

Even if you're older with less cash, it's still possible to do well, as a $0 initial investment and $500 in monthly investments at 10% per year turn into a total value of roughly $350,000. After 20 years, that won't be enough to buy a house on Lake Tahoe, but it will go a long way in addition to Social Security and/or any other financial inflows someone might have.

All things considered, consistent investing gets the job done.

The problem is turning a theory into a practice. I also know that consistently going to the gym is good. However, actually doing it and finding the right exercises is where it gets much harder.

So, in general, America's millionaires are right, as buying ETFs (or other passive investments) is a great way to build wealth.

  • By buying ETFs, we don't have to spend time picking stocks. We can use this time to improve our performance at work or for other stuff.
  • ETFs cannot go to zero. Single stocks, however, can.

Moreover, ETFs are a great way to follow the dividend growth strategy I discussed in an article titled “Dividend Growth Investing: The Strategy That Always Wins.”

Historically speaking, consistent dividend growth stocks outperform the market, supported by less weakness during bear markets and a decent performance during bull markets.

This consistent outperformance is based on a number of (related) factors:

  • Stable Earnings: Companies with consistent dividend growth often have reliable and predictable profits. This stability attracts investors seeking dependability — which applies to many different investor profiles.
  • Strong Fundamentals: Regularly increasing dividends signal strong fundamentals, such as solid balance sheets and healthy cash flows. This makes the companies more attractive to long-term investors. While some stocks may have faster growth, the underlying business models aren't always reliable.
  • Inflation Protection: Dividend growth can help protect income against inflation. As dividends increase, the purchasing power of investors remains strong — and often even rises net of inflation.
  • Management Confidence: Consistent dividend growth reflects management's confidence in the company's growth potential. Essentially, it signals that the company expects continued growth and stability in cash flows.
  • Commitment to Shareholders: Related to the point above, regularly increasing dividends shows a commitment to returning value to shareholders. It indicates that the company prioritizes rewarding its investors.

That's also where the 1976 paper from Michael Jensen and William Mechling comes in, as they discussed the agency theory.

As part of their findings, it can be said that if a company pays a dividend, the interests of managers and shareholders are aligned, which often results in better firm performance. Moreover, by paying a dividend, the amount of cash that can be used for other purposes is relatively lower. This pressures managers to make the most out of every dollar. It tends to improve efficiencies and capital returns.

This is why every penny of my post-spending capital goes into dividend stocks, as I believe the risk/reward for long-term investors cannot get better.

With that said, investors (like me) who do not buy ETFs need to take care of a wide range of issues.

For starters, I always invest with a super-long-term mindset, as I only add new companies that I wouldn't mind owning forever. For that, I like to use what I call an “ownership” view. While I only buy a very small part of a massive corporation, in my head, I pretend I own a big chunk of it. This mindset makes it easier to deal with issues, as I always ask, “How does this impact its long-term growth outlook and business model?”

This question is the reason I did not sell Norfolk Southern (NSC) when it ran into massive operating issues after the pandemic, or why I kept Deere & Company (DE) despite severe cyclical headwinds recently. I knew Norfolk had what it took to improve its business and that Deere would recover as soon as agriculture crop prices were to rise again.

As both are recovering (Deere just made a new all-time high), I'm glad I accepted a few years of headwinds.

The same goes for my investments in America's biggest defense contractors. All of them have been through some headwinds. However, as I discussed in a very detailed recent article, none of the current headwinds can derail the bull case, which is why I used the opportunity to add to some of my favorite dividend growers in the industry.

This brings me to the next part. All the companies I just mentioned have one thing in common: they have business moats to fend off competitors.

While I own some no-moat business companies in areas like real estate, even these companies have a specific advantage that makes them stand out. In the case of Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), it's its position in Southern California, a real estate market with high demand and severely constrained supply growth. Meanwhile, Extra Space Storage (EXR) has scaling power and pricing power due to mostly short-term leases.

The idea of a moat is very straightforward, which is why it's often abused. I have to admit that I have abused it as well in the past, as not every positive aspect of a company is a moat.

Just like Castle De Haar in the Netherlands (see below), a moat is used to fend off enemies (competitors) — the wider the moat, the better the survivability.

There are a number of factors I look for when assessing a company's moat:

  • Brand Loyalty: Some brands are preferred by customers. These can be consumer brands like Pepsi or Mercedes-Benz. I know people who would always stick to their car brand, even if other brands were objectively better and cheaper.
  • Cost Advantages: Companies that can produce goods or services more cheaply than competitors due to economies of scale or proprietary technology have an edge that is difficult to break. For example, some oil companies are so efficient that they can maintain production when others are forced to cut output. This also includes landowners like LandBridge (LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL), which generate royalties from oil and gas production. They are basically profitable when oil trades close to zero. The same goes for Moroccan phosphate fertilizer producers, as they have the cheapest resources, which allows them to impact supply and pricing in an industry with very low barriers to entry.
  • Network Effects: The value of a product or service increases as more people use it, like social media platforms or payment networks. This also includes companies like Deere & Company, which collects a lot of farm data to improve its services. It also applies to my investment in CME Group (CME), as the higher the volume of people trading on its futures exchanges, the more data it can gather and the more suitable services it can offer to data customers.
  • Switching Costs: This includes high costs or inconvenience for customers when switching to a competitor. I am a casualty of this, as I am mainly using Apple (AAPL) products. The thought of how much I need to change when switching alone keeps me from even considering a different brand.
  • Intellectual Property: Patents, trademarks, and proprietary technology that protect a company's products from being copied. This applies to many. It doesn't always result in a moat, but it's critical to have proprietary/protected products and services.

The VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat ETF (MOAT), which tracks wide moat stocks, has beaten both the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) since its inception.

While this is not a call to get anyone to buy MOAT, I believe the focus on moats is often neglected — especially when people assess younger and smaller companies.

In 2020/2021, for example, plenty of investors jumped into unprofitable tech companies, which are represented by the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) below. This resulted in high returns before the party ended abruptly. That's why I enjoy playing the long game, even if it sometimes means missing out on short-term gains.

Moreover, I do not make exceptions anymore when it comes to buying financially healthy companies anymore. Especially if rates remain “higher for longer” companies with poor financial health may be in a tough spot.

Speaking of my investments, if you want a full overview of my portfolio, I recently published an article showing my 23 holdings.

With that said, almost all articles I write on Seeking Alpha discuss investments that bring something special to the table. More often than not, this includes “Big Picture” developments, including macroeconomic changes, geopolitical developments, and related issues that cause market movement.

However, and this is critical, I stopped investing based on expected events a long time ago. I cannot reiterate enough how important it is to focus mainly on long-term shareholder generation instead of the goal of benefiting from certain shorter-term trends.

For example, regarding my defense stocks, if you had bought America's largest pure-play defense contractor, Lockheed Martin (LMT), to benefit from the war in Ukraine and elevated uncertainty between China and Thailand/Israel and Hamas, you would not have barely made any money.

Another good example is AI. NVIDIA (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are both GPU-focused semiconductor companies. Over the past three years, one of them returned close to 470%. The other remained flat. While I'm not saying buying Nvidia because of AI was a bad idea, it would have been a bad idea if investors had made major changes in their portfolios to make very specific bets, as “event-based” investing is very challenging.

That's why I bought LandBridge and Texas Pacific Land, as I explained in this article. In addition to making money from regular operations in oil and gas through resource sales and royalties, they own land in a remote area with nearly unlimited cooling water, dirt-cheap natural gas, and good internet connectivity. This is perfect for data centers.

In fact, I believe both TPL and LB can solve the bottleneck problem faced by the biggest tech companies, which is data center buildout constraints.

The best thing is that I can “bet on it” without having to take on elevated risks or turn my portfolio into an AI bet. Even if none of them ever get another data center (LandBridge is already working with a customer), I would remain bullish.

I do the same with defense contractors. While I benefit from shorter-term spikes in global geopolitical uncertainty, the main thesis is always to benefit from consistent dividend growth and buybacks — all fueled by innovation and wide-moat business models.

Essentially, that's why I always say I have a “win-win” strategy.

  • If my stocks fall, I can buy more at better prices. I win.
  • If my stocks go up, my net worth goes up. I win.

Moreover, since I started to get serious about dividend investing in 2020, I have never sold a stock at a loss. In other words, I was never forced to sell something, as all of my “divestitures” were strategic. This includes consolidation or the switch from Public Storage (PSA) to Rexford Industrial that I made last year.

In other words, I am perfectly fine with buying cyclical companies, as long as they are capable of consistent value creation, which makes them great compounders over time. Even better, if I know certain stocks are cyclical, I can use that information to my benefit and buy them during periods of cyclical weakness.

That's why I have been buying cyclical stocks since last year.

My Strategy In A Nutshell

In essence, my strategy revolves around disciplined, long-term investing with a focus on dividend growth and wide-moat companies.

By prioritizing consistent, regular investments in financially healthy businesses, I aim to build wealth steadily over time, avoiding the pitfalls of market timing and speculative bets.

Moreover, I invest with an “ownership mindset,” treating each investment as if I were acquiring a significant stake in the company, which helps me stay committed during periods of volatility or cyclical downturns. It also forces me to better understand the businesses, which is a great way to reduce risks.

Adding to that, my portfolio is built on the foundation of diversification, dividend growth, and companies with durable competitive advantages to make sure that I benefit from both capital appreciation and income generation.

That way, I have both income and growth, a strategy that could potentially allow me to retire without selling any stock. As I have a “never sell” mindset and no interest in retiring from the work that I love, it's the perfect strategy for me.

Essentially, whether the market rises or falls, I maintain a “win-win” approach: buying more at lower prices during dips and enjoying the upside during rallies.

This strategy not only aligns with the habits of self-made millionaires but also allows me to sleep well at night, knowing that my investments are built to withstand the test of time.

While it sometimes may sound like clickbait, I believe my strategy is one of the best ways to become a millionaire without having to take any excessive risks.

Risks To My Thesis

There are also some risks, as stock market returns do not come without pitfalls.

For example, economic downturns, rising rates, or sector-specific challenges could pressure even resilient businesses, impacting dividends and performance.

Also, misjudging competitive advantages or overconcentration in cyclical industries also poses risks, alongside inflation, regulatory changes, and geopolitical uncertainties that could disrupt cash flows and profitability.

If you want to avoid these risks, ETFs are for you.

data.title: The Reason Bitcoin Is A Terrible Investment
data.description:
Introduction

Bitcoin has been of great interest in the finance community over the past few years. While some people trade it, most investors use a buy-and-hold approach via ETFs such as ProShares Bitcoin ETF (NYSEARCA:BITO). In the following article, I will show why this approach is very unlikely to continue to work over the long term, the main reason being that if Bitcoin is treated as digital gold it means that the crypto market capitalization is capped at the current one gold is trading at. This limits the potential future returns that become far too low for the risk one is taking by investing in BITO.

ETF Analysis BITO

BITO is an ETF that invests in Bitcoin futures contracts. The ETF's expense ratio is 0.95%, almost double the average ETF fee. This also leads to a Seeking Alpha quant ratio of D- in this category.

What is also interesting to see is that BITO has 54% of its holdings in treasury bills, which gives it the ability to buy low when the market falls. However, since investors can do this by themselves, it rather is a disadvantage as investors could act more flexibly when deciding how much to hold in cash by themselves.

Another factor that makes BITO look bad is performance. Over the past year, the ETF showed a performance of -7.9% while comparable ETFs such as GBTC (Grayscale Bitcoin Trust BTC) yielded +66.72%. Although BITO is a futures-based ETF and GBTC is a spot ETF, both aim at replicating the performance of Bitcoin. Further on, the performance difference bears the question of why investors should buy a derivatives-based ETF if they might as well buy the underlying directly via GBTC for better performance. All of this shows that the ETF strategy is not just inflexible but also not performing well.

What is Bitcoin

Another factor for the performance of the ETF is its underlying. Before one can judge the future returns of BITO, it is important to classify which asset class Bitcoin belongs to.

One assumption many people make is that Bitcoin is an asset, such as stocks. However, as it is not productive and therefore has no intrinsic value; what you will get for it only depends on what the person you're selling it to is willing to pay.

This brings us right to the next assumption, which is that Bitcoin is like gold. This argument does make sense as both gold and Bitcoin are deflationary and have a limited supply. Also, the narrative of Bitcoin as a hard reserve for states is more and more pushed, putting it in the same position gold is in.

The fact that most people say cryptocurrencies does by itself imply that Bitcoin is exactly that. Since there is no way to effectively value a currency, we have to take a look at whether Bitcoin could be a good currency.

Most people support Bitcoin since it is deflationary, which means that their savings would rise in value over time. However, there is one core problem with this idea. While a deflationary currency is good for private savings, it is terrible for the economy. As an example, if you put your savings into a bank account at a rate of deflation of 2%, you will effectively earn the current rate of interest plus 2 percent. This leads to more money not being invested and makes credit conditions harder, which leads to rising interest rates for corporate bonds since they have to compete for a limited supply of credit. On top of higher interest rates, companies also have to pay the 2% deflation rate per year as the money gains value over time and has to be paid back later. Because of this, companies could do fewer projects, leading to less employment.

Almost every deflationary phase in history was one where people lost their jobs, companies went bankrupt, the economy shrank and the overall standard of living fell.

An additional factor that would further act against growth is interest rates. Since Bitcoin is very volatile, one would have to demand higher interest rates to lend to it. Further on, even if Bitcoin volatility goes down, one has to demand the rate of deflation on top of normal interest rates to get a better deal for lending out money than for just putting it in one bank account.

A last risk would also be that countries could go bankrupt if they lend in Bitcoin. The reason for this is that as long as countries have control over how much money they print, they can always print more to cover their debts. Although this leads to inflation, it's less worse than deflationary effects, since having a lower income is still better than having no income since you lost your job.

As you can see, Bitcoin would be a terrible currency that would slow down the economy of every country introducing it.

Estimating BITO's Future Returns

As we have seen, Bitcoin is not an asset like stocks. Also, it would be a terrible currency and should never be used on a large scale. This leaves the comparison to gold, which is also how it is currently used in the Bitcoin reserves the Trump administration wants to introduce.

The current market capitalization of gold is around US$15 trillion. This should be how big the crypto sector can get if it reaches the status of gold (in fact, it should be even smaller as it would have to share the spot with gold. Further on, gold has some real-world use cases which gives it additional value). Meanwhile, the market cap of the whole crypto sector is US$ 3.15 trillion, according to TradingView. The reason that Bitcoins returns should not go above the market capitalization of gold is that it has no productive use apart from that of gold, meaning it can only take gold's place or share gold's place as a "second gold".

Considering that gold has historically gained by as much as the rate of inflation which was 2% to 3%, one can calculate the returns of Bitcoin over future periods by calculating the yearly return Bitcoin would have to achieve to reach the market capitalization of gold in a given amount of years and adding the 3% average annual return of gold on top (since gold will likely increase its market capitalization in the given time).

You can see the results in Figure 1, where the best-case scenario means that crypto will reach the market cap of gold and where shared reserve currency means that it will reach half the combined value of gold and crypto (I assume a constant dominance of Bitcoin in the crypto sector). The years represent how long it takes crypto to reach the targeted level of market capitalization.

While crypto has shown significant returns over the years on its way to being a "digital gold", it almost has reached the heights of its usefulness as it should not be worth more than gold currently is.

As you can see, even if it takes Bitcoin only 10 years to reach the full market cap of gold, you still only have an annualized 13.8% return. At a 30-year time frame for the best-case scenario, returns will be lower than the historical return of 10% that the S&P 500 index achieved. This makes Bitcoin a terrible investment since one has to take almost four times the risks to achieve this return as can also be seen in Figure 2, comparing the standard deviation of SPY (SPDR S&P 500 ETF) and BITO.

Conclusion

While performing a buy-and-hold approach on Bitcoin using BITO might be interesting for diversification, the strategy's best days seem to be over if it is seen as a kind of reserve currency. Even in best-case scenarios, the expected annualized return is terrible, considering the very high risks investors have to take to get it. While buying BITO for the long term might not be a good idea, trading Bitcoin could still be beneficial due to the high volatility.

data.title: Etsy: The Era Of Shopping For Trinkets Is Swiftly Declining
data.description:
Amid a sky-high stock market that has swiftly shaken off early 2025’s fears of DeepSeek AI and the Fed’s pause in rate cuts, the macroeconomic picture still appears challenged, particularly for companies relying on discretionary consumer spending. So far this earnings season, many companies have reported disappointing holiday sales.

Joining that chorus is Etsy (NASDAQ:ETSY), the arts and crafts marketplace that enjoyed a brief resurgence during the pandemic - as did all e-commerce names. Since then, Etsy has dealt with persistent declines in GMS (gross merchandise sales, the total value of all goods sold on Etsy’s platform) - which has brought the stock price down with it. Etsy’s recently reported Q4 results showed a multiyear low in GMS declines, with the company’s outlook pointing to continued deterioration - leading the stock down a further ~10% as a result.

I last wrote a bearish opinion on Etsy in December, when the stock was still trading just shy of $60. Now, amid worsening marketplace performance metrics and a tough overall macro picture, I’m reiterating my sell rating on Etsy.

To Etsy’s credit, the company isn’t taking its declines laying down. It has invested significantly in expanding its feature set and showcasing certain types of products that are catching shoppers’ attention. In FY24, the company showcased its Gifting abilities, and GMS from Gifting (including Etsy gift cards) grew to encompass 29% of GMS. In FY25, the company is shifting its focus to Personalization, as it says a third of its shopping demand comes from people looking for personalized items.

Still - I don’t find major promise in any of these initiatives, as we note that Etsy’s revamp of its gifting capabilities was still unable to reverse sharp declines during the all-important holiday quarter. We also continue to be frustrated about the fact that Etsy’s marketing expenses are rising, both in nominal terms and as a percentage of revenue/GMs - and despite higher spend, the company seems to be unable to stimulate better demand trends.

All in all, I view Etsy as a lost cause. Declines in GMS should be a strong signal to dump this stock and invest elsewhere.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Etsy's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings highlights are shown in the slide below:

The headline figure: Etsy's GMS declined -6.8% y/y to $3.7 billion, which marked 270bps of deceleration versus a more modest -4.1% y/y decline in Q3. The company has dedicated a lot of resources to boosting performance marketing and to emphasizing the gifting abilities on its platform - and yet, it has still failed to produce growth in the all-important holiday quarter.

The company still touts that it's growing revenue, albeit at an anemic 1.2% y/y pace. We note that despite revenue growth, Etsy's $852.2 million of revenue missed Wall Street's expectations of $861.4 million (+2.3% y/y) by a one-point margin, while also decelerating quite sharply from 4.1% y/y growth in Q3.

The company's growth in revenue against a declining GMS is the result of a greater contribution from Etsy Ads (where sellers pay to promote their listings) and greater penetration of payments processing services, alongside a small incremental contribution generated from Etsy's new seller set-up fees (a $15 upfront one-time charge, which the company defends as helping Etsy to verify the authenticity of new sellers).

Still, in my view, these "growth drivers" are not levers that can be pulled in perpetuity. There is only so much ad density that Etsy can put on its platform, and increasing pricing and fees to boost take rates risks alienating the sellers on Etsy's platform (which have generally been quite vocal about price increases in the past). The only sustainable lever for long-term growth is a return to GMS growth.

Unfortunately, it doesn't appear as it this hope will become reality anytime soon. Etsy's Q1 outlook calls for GMS to decline at "a rate similar to the year-over-year performance reported for Q4'24," with take rates also remaining stable around ~23% - in other words, Etsy is expecting poor performance to continue.

Again, it's worth emphasizing that Etsy's poor top-line performance comes amid a jump in marketing spend. Etsy's total marketing spend in Q4 increased 9% y/y to $285.2 million, or 33.5% of revenue: 250bps higher than the year-ago period.

Also worth mentioning is that Etsy has dramatically shifted its advertising budget to performance marketing instead of brand marketing (performance marketing incentivizes immediate click-through actions, intended to drive immediate sales; whereas brand marketing is meant to reinforce brand messaging and its stickiness in consumers' minds). And yet, despite the substantial 22% y/y jump in performance marketing, the company still suffered decelerating GMS trends - which to me is an indication that the notion of purchasing handmade crafts and trinkets online is now antiquated. People may still buy clever handmade goods - but usually as impulse purchases, perhaps when traveling as a souvenir: but fewer people are willing to spend money to search for these items online.

We note that Etsy was still able to generate 6% y/y growth in adjusted EBITDA to $250.6 million, or a 29.4% margin, 140bps better y/y. However, its outlook calls for margins of 25-26% in Q1, which at the midpoint is 50bps worse than 26.0% in Q1'24.

Valuation and key takeaways

At the very least, Etsy has the semblance of a value stock. At current post-earnings share prices near $52, Etsy trades at a market cap of $5.83 billion. After we net off the $1.15 billion of cash and $2.29 billion of debt on Etsy's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $6.97 billion.

For the current fiscal year FY25, Wall Street analysts are expecting Etsy to generate $2.89 billion in revenue, or 2% y/y growth - roughly in line with Etsy's Q4 performance and what it has guided to for Q1, which assumes a continuation of Q4 y/y trends. If we optimistically assume adjusted EBITDA margins of 27.8% for FY25 against this revenue profile, flat to FY24 (recall that Etsy guided to a high end of flat margin performance in Q1, with the possibility for margins to drag as much as 100bps lower), adjusted EBITDA would be $803.4 million.

This puts Etsy's valuation at 8.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA - which stands out as cheap amid record-high stock markets that are still trading at a low ~20s multiple of earnings. That being said, with persistent GMS declines that have no end in sight, Etsy has all the makings of a value trap as it loses buyers and struggles to maintain revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Cinemark: Earnings Rebound Potential Lies In Popcorn
data.description:
Cinemark Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:CNK) operates movie theaters across the US and in several South American markets with 304 US theaters and 193 international theaters at the end of 2024, near-stable from a year ago with a slight decline in total screens. The company reported its Q4 results on the 19th of February, showing the impact of improving box office activity. On top, Cinemark has successfully expanded concession sales as popcorn and drinks are becoming a very critical earnings component.

In my previous August 2024 article on the stock, “Cinemark: Waiting For Better Box Office Activity”, I downgraded my rating on Cinemark to Hold as the stock valuation fairly reflected the company’s earnings rebound potential with eventually better box office activity. As the box office pipeline has improved, Cinemark’s stock has returned 8% compared to the S&P 500’s return of 11% after my previous article was published, as the stock is down -13% to weaker-than-expected Q4 profitability at the time of writing.

Cinemark Q4: Box Office Activity Has Improved, Earnings Rebound Lies in Popcorn Sales

Cinemark’s Q4 sales came in above expectations. The company’s revenues reached $814.3 million with rapid year-on-year growth of 27.5%, beating Wall Street’s consensus by $16.6 million. Revenues now also came in slightly above the Q4/2019 level prior to the Covid pandemic’s financial impact, following an also strong Q3 performance.

An improving box office backdrop contributed the most to Cinemark’s good performance – during Q4, there were notable high-grossing movie releases such as Wicked, Moana 2, Sonic the Hedgehog 3, Mufasa: The Lion King, and Gladiator II among other titles, driving healthier traffic to movie theaters as the industry is moving past low box office results in past years, caused by the Covid pandemic and Hollywood’s strikes.

On top of healthier box office activity, Cinemark’s company-specific performance has been good. Cinemark notes that the company exceeded the North American box office industry growth by 300 basis points in 2024, and by 100 basis points in international admissions against Latin America’s industrywide admissions – while Cinemark hasn’t notably grown its movie theater network, competitors have closed down a significant number of theaters, leading to the company’s slow market share gains against declining competition. Cinemark’s average ticket prices increased slightly in the US to $10.39 but declined slightly in the international segment to $3.68.

Notably, the company also increased concession sales penetration in the US, with popcorn and other high-margin snacks and drinks being an increasingly critical factor for healthy earnings. On top of improving attendance, concession revenue per paying US customer increased to $7.97 in Q4 from $7.67 a year ago, while international segment concession sales remained stable at $2.92 per customer. At an incredibly high 80.8% gross margin, concession sales generated $253.1 million in additional gross profit during Q4, up $57.4 million from a year ago.

Ultimately, the growing attendance and improved concession sales grew Cinemark’s Q4 operating income to $95.3 million from $20.2 million a year ago through rapid operating leverage. The increase in concession gross profit even makes up the majority of Cinemark’s year-on-year earnings increase, as concessions are increasingly becoming the main way to capitalize on movie theater traffic.

Despite the improving profitability, Cinemark's Q4 EPS of $0.33 missed Wall Street's consensus by $0.08 - Wall Street expected even more rapid earnings expansion from healthier sales as the stock's stark reaction also suggests. I believe that the report was still relatively good despite a very negative market reaction.

2025 Box Office Outlook Remains Healthy, But Q1 Could Be Slow

After Q4, Cinemark’s outlook for 2025 seems good. The company doesn’t provide forward financial guidance, but notes toward improved box office activity for the year – the year has notable movie releases such as Captain America: Brave New World, Minecraft the Movie, Jurassic World Rebirth, Zootopia 2, and many others driving growth in industry-wide attendance with box office grossing growth. Initial film slates for 2026 also look to drive an industry recovery after Covid lockdowns and the Hollywood strikes. Box office activity has remained subdued even in 2024, leaving much room for a further recovery in industry activity, growing Cinemark’s earnings well through operating leverage as the Q4 financials show.

So far, Q1 doesn’t seem to have had very high-grossing box office titles, contributing to a likely still quite slow Q1 performance. The US consumer sentiment has slowed down in early 2025, also potentially pressuring Cinemark’s traffic trends in the short term. Still, latter quarters in 2025 are likely to have much higher-grossing title releases as Cinemark looks to sustainably return to more stable admission trends after severe industry disruption. Notable quarterly fluctuation is still likely even within normal industry conditions, mainly depending on Hollywood’s release timeline.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Cinemark’s stock. After a good end to 2024, I estimate revenues to still climb 12% in 2025 and for box office activity to still contribute towards elevated growth in the 2026-2028 period, combining with Cinemark’s good expansion in concession sales.

I again estimate the EBIT margin to stabilize at 13.9%, being Cinemark’s 2003-2019 average, and for the cash flow conversion to be good as Cinemark isn’t notably expanding the company’s movie theater presence.

I now factor in the convertible notes to be converted, lowering debt by around $710 million but diluting outstanding shares by around 32 million effectively when including the impact of Cinemark’s dilution hedge transactions.

The updated estimates put Cinemark’s fair value estimate at $33.47, 17% above the stock price at the time of writing - the valuation now has a good margin of safety in the base financial scenario, providing a slight upside after the stock's decline to the Q4 report.

The fair value estimate is up from $27.15 previously due to slightly higher earnings estimates as well as a lower WACC.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 8.58%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.00% interest rate, annualized from the most recent quarter’s interest expenses and debt. I again estimate a fair 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.58% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I now update the beta estimate significantly lower as box office activity seems to have stabilized; I use Aswath Damodaran’s 0.89 unlevered beta estimate for the recreation industry, levered to the 30% debt-to-equity ratio for a final beta of 1.09. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 9.80% and the WACC at 8.58%.

Takeaway

Cinemark’s financial outlook is improving with better box office activity – the Q4 sales performance was good, and 2025 has several highly anticipated titles releasing despite a quieter Q1 so far. On top, Cinemark has successfully expanded concession sales in the US, becoming an even more critical earnings component in the industry's recovery. The improving earnings outlook is valued with a fair margin of safety, and as such, I keep my rating on Cinemark at Hold.

data.title: Pool Corp.: Should Investors Dip Their Toes? Not Yet
data.description:
Intro

Pool Corporation (NASDAQ:POOL) is a company that provides pool cleaning and maintenance tools, equipment, chemicals, and testing products. They are headquartered in Louisiana and offer a full-suite of components and materials needed to build, operate, and maintain pools.

Macro Factors & POOL Company Analysis

The company's annual filing does a good job of identifying their growth factors. Specifically, POOL aims to increase their penetration into households with the discretionary income and physical capacity to install a swimming pool. Additionally, POOL forecasts significant growth opportunities within pool remodels, equipment overhauls, and intensive, aesthetic-focused builds. From their 10-K, POOL has outlined several favorable trends that should positively impact their business. They are below:

  • Long-term growth in housing units in warmer climate markets due to the population migration towards the southern United States, which increases the demographic population that uses outdoor products like pools

  • Increased homeowner spending on outdoor living spaces for relaxation and entertainment, especially with consumers in more affluent areas

  • Consumers who bundle pool purchases / renovations with other general outdoor landscaping work, including irrigation, solar, and general outdoor home remodeling

  • Consumers increasingly focusing on environmentally sustainable, energy-efficient products

  • Permanent shift in the increase of remote work, leading to many individuals investing more in the comfort, aesthetics, and attractiveness of their home

POOL estimates that over 60% of consumer spending in the industry is on maintenance and repair work, and this helps to pad their bottom line. From the chart below taken from their 10-K, there has been a steady climb of in-ground pools in the US, and based on the factors mentioned earlier, this trend appears to be holding steady.

POOL has also proven to be open to acquisitions to power growth, as evidenced by their purchase of the Pinch a Penny pool store. Forecasted storefronts are expected to double through 2025, with a focus on Westward expansion to capture more areas with hot, desert climates. The below chart from the 2024 investor day highlights this growth area.

Through their POOL, which currently only provides 5% of POOL's total revenue, the company is able to expand usage of their vertically integrated chemical plant to consolidate and streamline the supply of cleaning chemicals in their portfolio. Additionally, they have tripled the number of private-label pool maintenance products available to consumers, which increases their overall brand reach.

Another differentiating factor for POOL is their digital platform, Pool360. This is their one-stop shop for water testing, ordering supplies, billing, and automating routine maintenance tasks for their business clients who maintain pools. It allows technicians to plan routes, order materials, test water quality, and more. Additionally, it also serves as an online catalogue for vendors and maintenance teams to order pool, backyard, and outdoor related products.

We are particularly interested in Pool360 because the platform helps to transition the company from simply a pool builder and operator, to a marketplace that affiliated and non-affiliated contractors, builders, retailers, and buyers can use - significantly broadening the overall market and stickiness of the POOL brand. Revenues specifically from Pool360 are not broken out in the annual report, but we believe that this platform does serve as a differentiating factor for the company as it increases its exposure not only to the builders and owners of pools, but also for the maintenance crews and servicing providers. This can help POOL become recognized as an integral partner far beyond the initial build of the pool, and allows POOL to garner consistent revenues over the lifespan of a pool build.

Beyond the company itself, and outside of the aforementioned macro factors highlighting the increase in pools in the US, one additional point of importance that we want to mention is that a pool is often a straightforward way for homeowners to increase the value of their property. Sure, it can be an investment between $20,000 to over $100,000, but according to a study done by BankRate, the average home value increases ~7% with a new pool added. This investment with clear positive returns could increase in the future as homeowners identify ways to boost the value of their property. Lastly, as expected, in-ground pools, which is where POOL specializes, typically costs 12x more than above-ground pools, leading to higher maintenance and upkeep prices down the line - positive for POOL.

Financial Highlights

Income statement (revenue and NI growth), balance sheet (current ratio, debt to equity ratio), Cash flow statement (OCF, FCF)

Revenue and Profit

POOL has had a respectable revenue growth over the past 10 years, with a CAGR of 10.6% from FY 2014 through FY 2023. Over this period of time, POOL's revenue increased every year except for 2023 in which they posted a revenue decrease of 10.3% compared to the previous year. In their 2023 Q4 earnings call, management attributes the decline to a slow start to the season at the beginning of the year caused by weather, as well as a decline in new pool installments caused by high interest rates and recent inflation.

Through the last 10 years, POOL's gross margin has stayed quite steady, hovering between 28.6% and 31.3%. POOL's operating margin, however, has varied more dramatically, with the highest margin in the last 10 years being almost double the lowest (8.4% in 2014 vs 16.6% in 2022). Both margins generally followed a similar trend as revenue, with increases over the last 10 years until 2023 where there was a decline.

Overall, the revenue and margin growth numbers look pretty good, especially the huge growth in operating margin. However, the downward trend in 2023 could be concerning, especially because in the trailing 12 months (which includes Q4 2023) we are seeing the trend continue with lower revenue, gross margin and operating margin compared to FY 2023.

Balance Sheet Health

POOL's balance sheet is looking quite good upon first glance, with a current ratio of 2.39 and only $1.2 billion in total debt. However, their debt to equity ratio of 86.52% is slightly higher than we are normally comfortable with for a potential value investment such as the POOL. Typically, we would aim for a company with a debt to equity ratio of 0.5 or less, for "value" style investments. Additionally, the current ratio is a little misleading, as inventory makes up roughly 75% of POOL's current assets. The company's quick ratio (similar to the current ratio but excludes inventory) is 0.77 which paints a less optimistic picture than the current ratio.

Cash Flows

POOL's cash flows look good at a high level. They have increasing free cash flows from $376 million in FY 2020 to $828 million in FY 2023. However, in the trailing 12 months, free cash flows are down to only $564 million. Overall, as is the theme with other aspects of the company, the cash flow statement has some positive indicators but also some potential red flags.

Valuation

For our valuation calculation, we decided to go with a simple approach using POOL's historical free cash flow (FCF) growth rate and arrived at a per-share value of $399.

We started by taking the FCF growth since 2020 and calculated that it has been growing at a CAGR of 10.66%. We used that same growth to forecast the next 5 years and applied a terminal value based on an estimated long-term growth rate of 3%.

As the company has had steady growth with occasional dips and spikes in the revenue, we thought that as an average, keeping the growth rate consistent with historical CAGRs made sense. Also, while pool and pool related maintenance demand is increasing, we do not foresee a sharp increase or decrease overall in growth rates in the long term.

Once applying a discount rate of 8.0% (the company's current weighted average cost of capital), we arrive at a valuation of $15.2 billion. With ~38.06M shares outstanding, this constitutes a $399 share price and ~16% gain from current prices.

Investment Potential

Based on the macro factors, internal company analysis, decent financials, and our DCF output of 16.16% increase in intrinsic value, we believe that POOL is not an unsafe investment, but it certainly does not have the makings of a growth play (30%+ projected returns) either. Based on the DCF output, the opportunity cost of tying up capital with POOL for a ~16% return makes it slightly less enticing where we would not call it a "growth play". The overall company, and industry, appear stable and sound, but it lacks an exciting, game-changing catalyst that could make this a rapidly growing company.

Additionally, one overarching risk with POOL is that in the event of a prolonged economic downturn, a "non-essential" item such as a pool most likely will be the first to get removed from a homeowner's budget if money is tight. However, the company does pay out a ~1.4% yield on their dividend, and the dividend should be relatively safe as it constitutes roughly a 40% payout ratio. POOL has increased their dividend consistently since 2014, growing ~18.5% CAGR over the past decade. This is good news for investors contemplating POOL as a more stable, buy and hold investment while reaping dividend income. Therefore, we recommend this to be a "Hold" - the possibility of consistent dividend income could be tempting for long-term investors.

Summary

POOL has steady revenues, dividends, and relatively stable cash flows, but the lack of exciting upside catalysts makes us rank this a hold. It could be a component within a dividend income portfolio for investors with longer time horizons looking for more stable positions. Intrinsically, it does look to be slightly undervalued. Management also appears open to M&A and investments in technology to make this company more robust and expand the overall revenue opportunity - good signs for potential investors.

data.title: Surprise, Surprise: Two More REIT Buys
data.description:
This continues to be a good environment for investing in REITs. REITs do well when interest rates are stable or falling, and inflation is low.

Over 75% of investors expect at least one rate cut in the next 12 months, bringing the prime rate down to 4.25%.

Meanwhile, YoY (year-over-year) inflation, at 3.3%, is running below the no-risk rate on Treasuries. The 10-year note is yielding 4.5%, while the 2-year pays 4.3%. A retiree living on dividends can sit in Treasuries at no risk, and inch ahead slightly.

According to a note by Citigroup from December, REITs are expected to return 10% to 15% in 2025, helped by a favorable environment. If Citigroup is right, that 10-15% return would be more than double the no-risk rate, making REITs worth the gamble, particularly if you choose well.

Healthcare REITs and Industrials are off to a fast start this year.

Industrials are enjoying a rotation after a 15% selloff in 2024, despite stellar operating performance. Prologis, the leading company in this sector, has seen "an impressive acceleration in leasing activity following the U.S. election," and believes the industrials market is bottoming. Meanwhile, absorption (demand) is finally catching up to completions (supply), following a 2-year glut.

Healthcare REITs are being boosted by both increased income (Cost of Living Adjustments) for their senior housing and skilled nursing patients, and a 15-year low in construction starts on new facilities. As Hoya Capital noted recently,

Fueled by tailwinds from record-setting COLA adjustments in 2023 and 2024, rent growth across both Senior Housing and Skilled Nursing facilities remained historically strong in 2024, each rising by over 4%. Supply growth remains muted as well, with NIC noting that the rolling four-quarter average for construction starts sits at 1.0% of total inventory - the lowest since 2010.

MOBs (medical office buildings) have remained fundamentally sound while enduring 6 consecutive quarters in which completions outpaced absorption, but that trend has begun reversing, starting in Q2 2024.

This article highlights two well-positioned companies to buy right now: one an Industrial, the other a Healthcare REIT.

STAG Industrial

STAG Industrial (STAG) owns 591 warehouse facilities, totaling over $115 msf (million square feet), spread across 41 U.S. states, including all Tier 1 industrial real estate markets. The company is headquartered in Boston and went public as a REIT in 2011. Leasing spreads over the past 12 months have been in excess of 27%.

Only 20% of the U.S. construction pipeline is located in STAG's top 10 markets. While the average new facility under construction is .30 msf, the average STAG facility is less than half that size. So the supply picture is tilted in the company's favor.

Meanwhile, E-commerce continues to increase as a percentage of all retail sales, driving demand for warehouse space.

STAG Growth

Here are the 3-year growth figures for FFO (funds from operations) and TCFO (total cash from operations).

Metric 2021 2022 2023 2024 3-year CAGR
FFO (millions) $332 $401 $421 $458 --
FFO Growth % -- 20.8 5.0 8.8 11.32%
FFO per share $2.06 $2.40 $2.21 $2.48 --
FFO per share growth % -- 16.5 (-8.0) 12.2 6.38%
TCFO (millions) $336 $388 $391 $460 --
TCFO Growth % -- 15.5 0.8 17.6 11.04%

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium and author calculations

As you can see, STAG has consistently grown FFO and TCFO by an average of over 11.0%, which is pretty impressive. FFO/share growth comes in at a healthy but not spectacular 6.38%, due to a step back in 2023.

Management is guiding for same-store NOI growth of 5.25–5.50% in 2025. The company expects to make $500 - $700 million in acquisitions, at a healthy cap rate of 6.25–6.50%, and $100 - $150 million in dispositions.

Meanwhile, here is how the stock price has done over the past 3 twelve-month periods, compared to the REIT average as represented by the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ).

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-yr CAGR
STAG share price Feb. 17 $39.30 $35.46 $39.10 $35.06 --
STAG share price Gain % -- (-9.8) 10.3 (-10.3) (-3.73)%
VNQ share price Feb. 17 $102.63 $89.17 $84.37 $91.63 --
VNQ share price Gain % -- (-13.1) (-5.4) 8.6 (-3.71)%

Source: MarketWatch.com and author calculations

Although the VNQ and STAG have gone in opposite directions each of the past two years, their 3-year CAGR is almost identical, at 3.7%. Factoring in the yield, STAG investors and VNQ investors have basically just broken even over the past 36 months.

STAG Balance sheet

Here are the key balance sheet metrics.

Company Liquidity Ratio Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA Bond Rating
STAG 2.07 32% 5.3 BBB

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Schwab, and author calculations

With a sturdy liquidity ratio of 2.07, debt ratio of 32%, and debt/EBITDA ratio of 5.3, STAG's bond-rated balance sheet is in very good shape.

As of December 31, STAG had $36.3 million in cash on hand, and a total of $2.85 billion in long-term debt, held at a weighted average interest rate of 3.97%, with a weighted average maturity of 4.6 years.

Less than 10% of STAG's debt matures in 2025 and 2027, but more than 10% in 2026, and a formidable wall forms in 2028 and 2029.

STAG Dividend

Among Industrial REITs, only two out-yield STAG, yet the company's Dividend Safety rating from Seeking Alpha Premium is a rock-solid B. The 4.25% dividend yield for STAG is highly competitive with the no-risk rate on Treasuries.

Company Div. Yield 3-yr Div. Growth Div. Score Payout Div. Safety
STAG 4.25% 0.68% 4.34 60% B

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Dividend Score projects the Yield three years from now, on shares bought today, assuming the Dividend Growth rate remains unchanged.

STAG Valuation

Despite its relatively high dividend yield, solid growth prospects, and solid balance sheet, STAG is selling at just 14.1x Price/FFO '24, well below both the Industrial REIT and overall REIT averages.

Company Div. Score Price/FFO '24 Premium to NAV
STAG 4.34 14.1 (-12.3)%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, and author calculations

Bottom line: STAG

STAG is a prosperous company, striking a wonderful balance between double-digit growth on the one hand, and highly competitive yield on the other. With a yield that almost matches the no-risk rate on Treasuries, sturdy growth prospects, and a strong balance sheet, all at a nice price, STAG offers a good chance to earn some capital gains at very low risk.

Five Wall Street analysts rate STAG a Buy or Strong Buy, and none rate the company a Sell. The average price target is $38.92, implying 10.8% upside. Zacks rates STAG a Sell, however, and the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings system recently downgraded it from Buy to Hold, based on negative earnings revisions.

Omega Healthcare Investors

Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) provides triple-net leasing and fixed-rate mortgage loans for 86 skilled nursing and assisted living facilities in 41 U.S. states and the U.K., with 1000 properties totaling over 92,000 beds, all under third-party management.

The company is headquartered in Maryland, and went public in 1992. SNFs (skilled nursing facilities) comprise 70% of OHI's portfolio, while senior housing makes up the other 30%. Assets are most concentrated in the Sun Belt and up the Mississippi and Ohio River valleys into Pennsylvania.

In addition to being the most likely destination for seniors discharged from the hospital, SNFs are also the lowest-cost option for 24-hour nursing care.

Importantly, as the U.S. population ages and demand for SNF's increases, the supply has remained unchanged for many years, due mostly to regulatory restrictions. This puts upward pressure on occupancy rates. Currently, OHI's SNF portfolio enjoys only 80.9% occupancy. This should also rise as more and more Baby Boomers join the 75-or-older cohort.

Omega is quite a bit more adept than the average REIT at converting revenue into EBITDA.

In addition to being triple-net leases, the company's leases are also long-term, with an average term of 9.4 years, very few near-term expirations, and very low risk of lease turnover.

OHI's tenant diversification is pretty good. The top 2 tenants each account for 10% of ABR, but the top 10 together only account for 58%.

OHI Growth

Here are the 3-year growth figures for FFO (funds from operations) and TCFO (total cash from operations).

Metric 2021 2022 2023 2024 3-year CAGR
FFO (millions) $655 $460 $591 $734 --
FFO Growth % -- (-29.8) 28.5 24.2 3.87%
FFO per share $3.31 $2.71 $2.99 $2.88 --
FFO per share growth % -- (-18.1) 10.3 (-3.7) (-4.53)%
TCFO (millions) $722 $626 $618 $749 --
TCFO Growth % -- (-13.3) (-1.3) 21.2 1.23%

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

According to the company, despite its high dividend yield, OHI has managed to grow AFFO by an average annual rate of 6.5% since 2004. If that is indeed the case, then most of that growth must have happened more than 5 years ago. 2021 was a banner year for the company, but since then, 3-year CAGR for FFO has been sluggish at 3.87%, although it is notable that the last two years, that same growth rate has been in excess of 20%. Cash from operations has grown at just a 1.23% clip. Meanwhile, FFO per share has stepped backward by an average of (-4.53)% annually.

Meanwhile, here is how the stock price has done over the past 3 twelve-month periods, compared to the REIT average as represented by the Vanguard Real Estate ETF.

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-yr CAGR
OHI share price Feb. 17 $28.82 $28.23 $30.95 $36.40 --
OHI share price Gain % -- (-2.0) 9.6 17.6 8.09%
VNQ share price Feb. 17 102.63 89.17 84.37 91.63 --
VNQ share price Gain % -- (-13.1) (-5.4) 8.6 (-3.71)%

Source: MarketWatch.com and author calculations

OHI shares are riding a hot streak that has seen the share price spike over 25% in the past 24 months. Adding in the dividend yield, OHI investors have enjoyed total returns in the neighborhood of 15% per annum.

OHI Balance sheet

Here are the key balance sheet metrics. Liquidity and debt ratio are solid, and the balance sheet is bond rated. Debt/EBITDA is outstanding, at 4.7.

Company Liquidity Ratio Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA Bond Rating
OHI 1.92 32% 4.7 BBB-

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Of the company's $4.44 billion in debt, 5.1% is held at variable rates. OHI has about $1.44 billion available on its revolving line of credit. About 8% of OHI debt matures in 2025. The following two years, 2026 and 2027, present a formidable wall of debt, with 25% of the total load due in 2027 alone.

OHI Dividend

OHI's dividend yield of 7.36% stands out from the crowd, and the lack of dividend growth is not a concern. However, the company earns a Dividend Safety grade of D from Seeking Alpha Quant ratings, due almost entirely to its aggressive payout ratios. However, their payout ratios have historically been high, and their revenue streams so stable and predictable that I think the dividend is safer than the Quant grade would indicate.

Company Div. Yield 3-yr Div. Growth Div. Score Payout Div. Safety
OHI 7.36% 0.0% 7.36 90% D

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Dividend Score projects the Yield three years from now, on shares bought today, assuming the Dividend Growth rate remains unchanged.

OHI Valuation

OHI shares are selling far below the REIT average and Healthcare sector average, at 12.6x. Although Hoya Capital shows share selling at a 47% premium to NAV, the Healthcare sector average is much higher.

Company Div. Score Price/FFO '24 Premium to NAV
OHI 7.36 12.6 47.0%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital and author calculations

Bottom line: OHI

With a yield almost double the no-risk rate, and positive growth prospects, OHI seems like a good bet to reward investors who are willing to run a small risk on the share price over the next year or two.

Seven Wall Street analysts rate OHI a Buy or Strong Buy, and none rate it a Sell. Zacks and the Seeking Alpha Quant ratings system also name OHI a Buy.

The average Wall Street price target on OHI is $41.87, implying 15.2% upside.

data.title: Haemonetics: Almost Blood In The Streets
data.description:
In the spring of last year, I believed that appeal was improving, following another bolt-on deal in the case of Haemonetics (NYSE:HAE). The blood business has seen modest growth ever since, but shares have fallen, mostly in the wake of a recently announced reduction in the full year sales guidance.

This move has increased appeal quite a bit, making current levels start to look a lot more compelling to get involved.

A Blood Business

Haemonetics describes itself as an innovative medical technology solution provider, aiming to improve quality, effectiveness and efficiency of care. The company generates about $1.3 billion in annual sales with more than 3,000 team members.

The largest business is the plasma business, responsible for nearly half of sales from disposables, collection devices and donor management services. The hospital segment makes up about a third of sales, comprised out of interventional and blood management technologies. This is complemented by the blood center segment, the third and smallest segment of the business.

Some three quarters of the $1.3 billion revenue base are generated in the US, complemented by about a 12% contribution from Europe, with the remainder of sales generated in the wider Asian region. Revenues have come in at nearly a billion between 2015 and 2022, but amidst inflation, organic growth and M&A efforts, sales have advanced to $1.3 billion.

Following a $253 million deal for Canada-based medical device cardiology business OpSens in the fall of 2023, the company was on track to earn close to $4 per share on an adjusted basis for all of 2023. With shares trading at $80 in the spring of last year, expectations remained elevated at around 20 times earnings, while net debt of $836 million resulted in a leverage ratio around 3 times.

While organic growth improved, I was somewhat cautious as some of the business units had suboptimal positioning, with not all activities focused on growth engines yet.

Expectations Come In

In May of last year, the company reported a 12% increase in fiscal 2024 sales to $1.31 billion. The company posted adjusted earnings of $3.96 per share, with GAAP earnings reported at just $2.29 per share after no less than 12 adjustments. While I am happy to adjust for amortization costs, and some others, I pegged realistic earnings at just $3 and change per share.

The company provided somewhat of a comforting outlook for the fiscal year 2025, with total sales growth seen between 5-8%, with M&A efforts contributing about five points, and organic growth seen coming in between flat and positive 3%. This should result in earnings per share of around $4.60 per share, plus or minus fifteen cents.

During the fall, the company posted sound second quarter results, and while the full-year guidance was kept intact, it was supported by stronger organic growth, offset by weaker contribution from M&A efforts. By December, the company announced the divestment of the whole blood assets business to GVS S.p.A. in a deal valued at $67 million, of which roughly two-thirds upfront and the remainder due in earn-outs in the coming years.

Early in February, Haemonetics reported a 4% increase in third quarter sales to $349 million, as organic growth came in flat. Total revenue growth for the year was cut to 3-5% amidst intensifying currency headwinds, a 3-4% contribution from acquisitions (a point less than the previous guidance), with organic growth seen at 0-3% (also a point less than the previous guidance).

While the company maintained the midpoint of the full-year earnings guidance at $4.60 per share, the midpoint of the free cash flow guidance was cut by twenty-five million to $130 million, equal to just over $2.50 per share. Net debt is reported at $904 million, as leverage ticked up amidst poor net working capital generation, and share buybacks.

Coming Down

Following the pullback in the sales guidance and free cash flow guidance, shares of Haemonetics have fallen to $64 per share, as shares have largely traded in a $70-$90 range over the past year. This values the business at $3.3 billion, or $4.2 billion if we factor in net debt.

Based on a $1.4 billion revenue base, this values the company at around 3 times sales. Adjusted earnings multiples have fallen from 20 times in the spring of last year to just about 14 times, but these earnings are somewhat adjusted, of course.

Even if I see realistic earnings at $3.50-$4.00 per share, these shares traded at a reasonable 16-18 times earnings multiple with net debt down to 2.4 times EBITDA. This debt load will come down minimally following the divestment of Whole Blood, which involves some $49 million in revenues leaving the door, but is not having an impact on earnings per share.

Putting this into perspective, Haemonetics was a $40 stock already a decade ago, and with exception to volatility in a $100-$150 range in the pandemic era, shares have fallen quite a bit, trading at historically very reasonable levels.

Weighing this altogether, the company is undergoing a reasonable transition, although organic growth in recent times has been underwhelming. That said, modest advancements in the business, but moreover share price declines, have resulted in multiples becoming a lot more appealing here, with appeal readily arising here.

data.title: iQIYI: Struggling Against Mini-Drama Surge And Macro Headwinds
data.description:
iQIYI (NASDAQ:IQ) reported its 4Q24 results, with the topline missing the consensus estimate on the back of a very low expectation. The most concerning part is that the deterioration of fundamentals is evident in both membership and advertising segments. Membership revenue declined for the fifth consecutive quarter due to a lack of hit content, while advertising remains pressured amid China’s challenging macro backdrop.

Management continues to remain optimistic, citing the recovery in membership revenue in 1Q25 due to a better content slate, but we remain cautious as we believe competition from mini-drama is eroding IQ’s subscriber base and that lack of innovation in content development to counter the emerging popularity of mini-drama could pose a key risk to IQ in 2025.

Within the online media vertical, our preference is Netflix over IQ as we believe NFLX has a more established self-reinforcing model and flywheel effect that allows it to gain global scalability.

We value IQ at 0.8x EV/Revenue, in line with content producer Paramount, which trades at a similar valuation range. This would imply 6.5x forward earnings, in line with that of ad peer Weibo, which trades at this range, given IQ’s exposure to China’s ad spending environment. We see another 16% downside from the current level, and this stock could trend towards $2/share if there is no material recovery in membership and ad revenue growth.

IQ’s membership revenue declined by 15% y/y to RMB 4.1bn due to a lighter content slate. Management did note a sequential recovery in Q1 membership revenue driven by new content such as Drifting Away and Moonlight Mystique, but even if there is a sequential recovery, we expect the membership revenue to continue to decline in the high single-digit percentage on a y/y basis.

More concerning is the lack of full-year guidance as we wrap up two months of 2025, implying incremental uncertainty towards the membership revenue trajectory for the year. Instead, management provided some direction on the content lineup for 2025 by emphasizing the upcoming premium dramas including Breaking the Shadows, Justifiable Defense, A Life for a Life, Low IQ Crime, Fox Spirit Matchmaker: Sword and Beloved, A Dream Within a Dream, and What A Wonderful World. In addition, suspense and female-led drama will be two key categories as management thinks these programs could drive higher engagement.

For mini-dramas, IQ secured partnerships with 95% of the market's leading content providers and enhanced the ad placement system to monetize the traffic in this segment. The first mini-drama surpassed RMB 1mn in revenue in December, a small feat that underscores that this segment remains in its early days and that scalability will be a key focus going forward.

Our key concern regarding the mini-drama is that IQ may be pursuing quality over quantity to remain competitive. While this may drive near-term subscriber and revenue growth, long-term dilution of quality could ultimately revert to membership decline due to the proliferation of similar dramas on other platforms such as Tencent, ByteDance, and Kuaishou. We note that competition from ByteDance is heating up, with Douyin rolling out its own subscription model for mini-dramas priced as low as RMB 8/month and access to 4 K mini-dramas. Tencent is also focused on this area, given that Tencent Video is one of the leading OTA platforms in China. The proliferation of mini-dramas is likely to erode IQ’s membership revenue as subscribers churn, and this poses a key risk to IQ’s revenue growth for 2025.

On advertising, IQ reported an ad revenue decline of 13% y/y due to weakness in brand advertising, partially offset by growth in performance ads. Management stated that brand advertising showed double-digit sequential growth in Q4, supported by premium dramas. Still, the overall sector remains challenging due to competition from platforms such as Tencent, RedNote and Bilibili.

Similar to the other advertising platforms, this segment is subject to China’s macro backdrop. Without a material recovery in the economy, this segment could remain pressured as advertisers become increasingly conscious of the ad budget amid weak business and consumer sentiment.

All things considered, we don't believe that investors should buy into IQ after the post quarter sell-off. Our preference within the online streaming platform remains Netflix over IQ as we believe that IQ faces structural challenges that prevent it from establishing a self-reinforcing content and subscription model akin to Netflix.

While Netflix has successfully built a flywheel effect, where strong content drives subscriber growth, leading to higher revenue and reinvestment into more high-quality content, iQIYI struggles to achieve the same for several reasons:

First, IQ operates within China, where the company is faced with much tougher content restrictions and regulatory oversight compared with NFLX, which does not face similar restrictions.

Second, NFLX can leverage its global audience with a higher ARPU. IQ operates in a hypercompetitive domestic Chinese market with increasing price elasticity due to consumption downgrade and sentiment that is limiting IQ’s ARPU upside.

Third, unlike NFLX, which has moved into a hybrid subscription-advertising model with premium advertisers, iQIYI remains more dependent on ad revenues, which are under pressure due to China’s weak macroeconomic conditions, as we stated above. While NFLX has seen its ad-supported tiers enhance its monetization and flywheel effect, IQ is facing tougher competition from the likes of Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) and Bilibili (BILI) when attracting high-value advertisers.

Finally, IQ remains heavily dependent on the Chinese market, with only moderate success in overseas expansion compared with NFLX, which has built a global subs base with a diversifying revenue stream. Without substantial global reach, iQIYI lacks the scalability needed to fuel a long-term flywheel effect, in our view.

Given the number of challenges, we believe a 6.5x P/E or 0.7x EV/Revenue is a fair valuation for IQ based on a blended content and ad peer. This implies further downside to the current level and is best avoided, in our view.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Palantir Drops On Defense Cut Memo
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Palantir (PLTR) ends session in red as Hegseth memo calls for sharp spending cut. (00:23) Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) to deliver more planes this year, Spirit Aero (SPR) hurdles delay A350 freighter. (01:10) Amazon (AMZN) gives up on its Chime communications service. (02:31)

This is an abridged transcript.

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) fell more than 10% on Wednesday and is down nearly 2% in premarket action.

This after a memo obtained by the Washington Post from Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth called for an 8% cut to the defense budget over each of the next five years.

The news outlet added that the proposed cuts are required to be drawn up by Feb. 24. There are 17 categories that are said to be exempted from the cuts, including security at the southern U.S. border, the modernization of the U.S. nuclear weapon arsenal and missile defense, one-way attack drones and other areas.

News of the potential cuts were first reported over the weekend.

Palantir did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

—

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) is expecting to deliver more planes in 2025 compared to a year ago even as it faces continued supply chain challenges and broader uncertainty in light of global trade tensions.

The European aircraft maker expects to deliver around 820 commercial planes this year, slightly lower than the consensus estimate of 824 jets, but higher than 766 deliveries in 2024.

The outlook assumes no additional disruptions to global trade, air traffic, the supply chain, and Airbus' (OTCPK:EADSF) internal operations. It also excludes the impact of potential new tariffs on the company's business.

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) pushed the entry-into-service date for the A350 freighter variant to the second half of 2027, from its earlier target of 2026.

The company continues to target a monthly production rate of 12 A350s in 2028 and 14 A220s in 2026. The A320 family continues to ramp up towards a rate of 75 aircraft per month in 2027, while monthly A330 production is stabilizing at around rate of 4.

The plane maker is taking over some parts of Spirit Aerosystems (SPR) business, while rival Boeing (BA) is acquiring the majority of the business.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is giving up on its Chime communication service.

The company announced on Wednesday that it is withdrawing support for the Amazon Chime service next year and will stop accepting new customers today.

Organizations with at least one Amazon Chime Team or an enterprise account can continue to use Amazon Chime and Business Calling features until February 20, 2026, when all support for the service will end.

Amazon Chime was officially announced on February 13, 2017 and positioned itself as a competitor to established services like Skype for Business, Cisco WebEx, Google Hangouts, and LogMeIn's GoToMeeting.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Beyond Meat in talks with lenders to help shore up its liquidity - Bloomberg

Federal Reserve officials consider trade, immigration impacts: FOMC minutes

Carbon capture would boost U.S. energy independence, Occidental CEO says

Catalyst watch:

  • The CAGNY Conference will include presentations from Procter & Gamble (PG), US Foods (USFD), Diageo (DEO), and Clorox (CLX).

  • Walmart (WMT) will hold its earnings conference call. The retail giant's business updates have impacted shares of Target (TGT), Costco (COST), Dollar General (DG), Dollar Tree (DLTR), and Kroger (KR) in the past. Major suppliers with a strong post-earnings trading correlation with Walmart include Reynolds Consumer Products (REYN), TreeHouse Foods (THS), and Flower Foods (FLO).

  • Mobileye Global (MBLY) will present at the Barclays Industrial Select Conference.

  • Boeing (BA) CEO Kelly Ortberg will speak at the Barclays Industrial Select Conference.

  • Visa (V) will hold its Investor Day event.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $72/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1% at $97,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.3% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Shares of Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) dropped around 10% despite a strong Q4 performance, which saw the company beat top- and bottom-line estimates, increase car sales, and improve profit per vehicle.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 9:35 am Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Chicagoland Chamber Mid-Market Chicago event.

  • 12:05 p.m St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank President Alberto Musalem will speak and participate in a moderated conversation on the U.S. economy and monetary policy before the Economic Club of New York.

  • 2:30 pm Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr will speak on "Supervision and Regulation" at the Georgetown University Law Center.
